_comment: One file per procedure_code. Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/<condition>/ : _Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + _PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code.
alos: 5 days (Once diagnosis is established the case can be booked in the relevant package, further stay/admission should be decided based on the level of complications of the disease)
clinical_indications: Cyanotic breath-holding spells are often precipitated by anger or frustration, usually in response to a reprimand.
The child usually emits a short, loud cry, which leads to a sudden involuntary holding of the breath in forced expiration.
The child becomes rigid or limp followed by a transient loss of consciousness during which child turns blue.

preauth.label: Complete blood count, serum ferritin, blood glucose

claim.label: Detailed Indoor case papers (ICPs) with treatment details
Detailed Discharge summary with follow-up advise at the time of discharge

ppd_preauth.q: Is the age of onset and precipitating factors like frustration/anger/fear documented?
Is there a h/o of sudden choking, hoarseness of voice, limpness?

common_queries: Age of onset or precipitating factors (like frustration/anger/fear) not documented in clinical notes.
History of sudden choking, hoarseness of voice, or limpness not documented in clinical notes.
Echocardiography (2D ECHO) or ECG/ECHO report not provided as required (ECG/ECHO specifically if 2D ECHO is not available).

clinical_key_pointers: Cyanotic spells affect infants and young children from six months to five years of age. The onset is usually between 6 and 18 months of age and is almost always before two years of age.
Cyanotic breath-holding spells are often precipitated by anger or frustration, usually in response to a reprimand.
The child usually emits a short, loud cry, which leads to a sudden involuntary holding of the breath in forced expiration.
The child becomes rigid or limp followed by a transient loss of consciousness during which child turns blue.
